_meta.description: Judge calibration rubrics for dspy-code-hygiene. Edit the anchors to match YOUR quality standards. The judge uses these to ground its 0.0-1.0 scoring so it aligns with what you consider good code.

docstring.rubric:
Weighted criteria:
  0.30 - Correctness: accurately describes what the function does
  0.25 - Completeness: every parameter documented, return value described, exceptions listed
  0.20 - Style: Google format (Args/Returns/Raises), Oxford commas, no em dashes
  0.15 - Conciseness: no filler, no restating the function name as the summary
  0.10 - Usefulness: would a new developer understand the function from the docstring alone?
docstring.style_rules:
- Google style docstrings only (Args, Returns, Raises sections)
- Oxford commas in all lists
- No em dashes anywhere (use commas, parentheses, or separate sentences)
- First line is a verb phrase summarizing the function (not the function name)
- Args entries include type in description only if not annotated
- One blank line between sections

anchors.0.3: A one-line docstring that just restates the function name: 'Parse the file.' No Args, no Returns, no Raises.
anchors.0.5: Has an Args section but misses one parameter, no Returns section. Summary is okay but generic.
anchors.0.7: Complete Args and Returns sections, all params documented, Google style. Summary could be more specific. Minor style issues (missing Oxford comma).
anchors.0.9: Every param documented with clear descriptions, Returns explains the structure, Raises lists specific exceptions with when they occur. Summary is a precise verb phrase. Perfect style.
anchors.0.95: All of 0.9, plus the docstring helps you understand the function without reading the implementation. Descriptions include value ranges, edge case behavior, and default explanations.

type_annotation.rubric:
Weighted criteria:
  0.40 - Correctness: types accurately reflect runtime behavior
  0.25 - Completeness: every parameter and return value annotated
  0.20 - Precision: uses specific types (list[str]) not overly broad (Any)
  0.15 - Style: lowercase generics, union syntax (str | None not Optional[str])
type_annotation.style_rules:
- Lowercase generics: list[str] not List[str]
- Union syntax: str | None not Optional[str]
- Never use Any unless truly unconstrained
- Use specific collection types: dict[str, int] not dict
- Path | str for filesystem arguments
- Return None explicitly, never omit return type

anchors.0.3: Only 'return' annotated, all params bare. Uses capital List instead of list.
anchors.0.5: Most params annotated but one is 'Any' when it should be 'list[str]'. Return type correct.
anchors.0.7: All params and return annotated. Types are correct. Minor style: uses Optional instead of union.
anchors.0.95: Every type is precise and correct. Uses lowercase generics, union syntax, specific collection types. Could serve as the ground truth.

test_generation.rubric:
Weighted criteria:
  0.30 - Correctness: tests would actually pass against the implementation
  0.25 - Coverage: happy path, edge cases (empty, None, boundary), error cases
  0.20 - Assertions: meaningful asserts that verify behavior, not just 'doesn't crash'
  0.15 - Structure: proper use of parametrize, fixtures, descriptive test names
  0.10 - Independence: tests don't depend on each other or external state
test_generation.style_rules:
- Test names: test_<function>_<scenario> (e.g., test_parse_file_empty_input)
- Use assert statements, not unittest-style assertEqual
- pytest.raises for expected exceptions
- @pytest.mark.parametrize for multiple inputs to same logic
- Fixtures for complex setup, not inline construction
- No mocking unless necessary for external dependencies

anchors.0.3: Single test function, just calls the function and asserts it doesn't raise. No edge cases. Generic name like 'test_function'.
anchors.0.5: Happy path test with a real assertion. One edge case. Test names are descriptive. Missing error case testing.
anchors.0.7: Happy path, 2-3 edge cases (empty, None), one error case with pytest.raises. Parametrize for the happy path. All assertions are meaningful.
anchors.0.9: Comprehensive: happy path with parametrize, all edge cases (empty, None, boundary values, type mismatches), error cases with specific exception types. Fixtures for setup. Tests are independent.
anchors.0.95: All of 0.9, plus tests document the function's contract. Reading the tests tells you exactly what the function does, including corner cases the docstring doesn't mention.

complexity_reduction.rubric:
Weighted criteria:
  0.35 - Correctness: suggested refactoring preserves original behavior
  0.25 - Impact: would this actually reduce complexity (fewer branches, less nesting)?
  0.20 - Practicality: suggestion is specific enough to implement directly
  0.10 - Code quality: suggested code follows style rules
  0.10 - Prioritization: high-impact suggestions ranked first
complexity_reduction.style_rules:
- Guard clauses over nested if/else (early return pattern)
- Lookup tables over long if/elif chains
- Extract function when a block has a clear single responsibility
- Never suggest extractions for 2-line blocks that only run once
- Suggested code must be syntactically valid Python
- Preserve all error handling in refactored code

anchors.0.3: Generic advice like 'simplify this function'. No specific code, no line references. Could apply to any function.
anchors.0.5: Identifies the right problem (e.g., deep nesting) but suggested code has syntax errors or would change behavior.
anchors.0.7: Correct guard clause extraction with valid code. Targets the right lines. Would reduce nesting by 1-2 levels. Preserves behavior.
anchors.0.95: Multiple suggestions ordered by impact. Each has valid, tested code. Extractions create well-named helpers. Combined effect cuts cyclomatic complexity by 30%+. All error paths preserved.

refactoring.rubric:
Weighted criteria:
  0.35 - Correctness: extraction/rename preserves behavior and semantics
  0.25 - Naming: extracted function names are descriptive verb+noun, renames improve clarity
  0.20 - Boundary: extraction point is a clean seam (clear inputs/outputs)
  0.10 - Rationale: explanation makes sense to another developer
  0.10 - Scope: doesn't over-extract (pulling out 2-line helpers that only run once)
refactoring.style_rules:
- Extracted functions: verb_noun naming (e.g., classify_severity, build_entry)
- Variables: descriptive, no abbreviations except well-known (cfg, ctx, idx)
- Booleans: is_/has_/can_/should_ prefix
- Never rename loop variables (i, j, k) or math variables (x, y)
- Extraction must reduce the original function, not just move code

anchors.0.3: Suggests renaming 'i' to 'index' in a simple loop. Over-extracts a 2-line assignment into a separate function.
anchors.0.5: Identifies a real DRY violation but the extracted function name is vague ('process_data'). Boundary is unclear.
anchors.0.7: Clean extraction with descriptive name, clear inputs/outputs. Rename suggestions target genuinely confusing names. Rationale explains why.
anchors.0.95: Extractions create reusable, testable helpers. Names are precise. The refactored code reads like documentation. No over-extraction.

comments.rubric:
Weighted criteria:
  0.35 - Relevance: comments explain non-obvious logic, not obvious operations
  0.25 - Accuracy: comment correctly describes what the code does
  0.20 - Categorization: correct category (explanation/warning/todo/rationale)
  0.20 - Value-add: would removing this comment make the code harder to understand?
comments.style_rules:
- Never comment obvious operations (x = x + 1  # increment x)
- Focus on WHY, not WHAT (rationale over description)
- Warnings for non-obvious side effects or gotchas
- TODOs must be actionable (not 'TODO: fix this')
- Keep comments concise (one line when possible)
- Comment above the line, not inline (unless very short)

anchors.0.3: Comments obvious operations: '# loop through items' on a for loop. Wrong categories.
anchors.0.5: Some useful comments mixed with obvious ones. Categories mostly correct. A few are too vague.
anchors.0.7: All comments explain non-obvious logic. Categories correct. No obvious-operation comments. Concise.
anchors.0.95: Every comment answers a question the reader would have. Warnings flag real gotchas. Rationale comments explain design decisions that aren't obvious from the code. Zero noise.

missing_imports.rubric:
Weighted criteria:
  0.40 - Correctness: suggested import actually provides the used name
  0.30 - Confidence: only high-confidence suggestions (no guessing)
  0.20 - Specificity: from-import over bare import where appropriate
  0.10 - Ordering: follows isort conventions

anchors.0.3: Suggests 'import os' when the code uses 'Path' (wrong module). Low confidence on everything.
anchors.0.7: Correctly identifies 'from pathlib import Path' for Path usage. High confidence. Misses one obscure import.
anchors.0.95: Every suggestion is correct, high confidence, specific from-imports. Catches typing imports, stdlib, and third-party. No false positives.
